analysis:
Across the top heuristics (1–5) a clear pattern appears: fast deterministic Pareto prefilter → cheap per-point proxy ranking (box-volume, scalarizations) → small k‑proportional shortlist → CELF/lazy-greedy or greedy insertion using cached singleton HVs → selective pairwise caching when shortlist is tiny → bounded 1‑for‑1 local swaps → deterministic padding/tie‑breaking. Those designs minimize expensive hypervolume calls, reuse cached HV values, and keep the exact HV work confined to a small candidate set.

Weaker variants either (a) expand the shortlist too much or use very dense weight ensembles (many HV-relevant computations) causing heavy HV-call cost, (b) miss or underuse caches (repeated vstack/hv calls inside inner loops), or (c) rely on lots of Python-level looping or random sampling that increases overhead and nondeterminism. Notable avoidable overheads: repeated recomputation of reference diffs, frequent vstacking/copying, set/list membership checks in hot loops, and lack of an HV-call budget/early pruning. Good data reuse patterns are: singleton HV cache, optional pairwise cache for small shortlists, vectorized proxy computations (box and scalarization), and preallocated buffers to avoid repeated concatenation.

Practical tradeoffs: larger shortlists or denser scalar probes can improve selection quality but cost many HV evaluations; CELF lazy updates and upper‑bound pruning (box-volume or singleton upper bounds) give a favorable quality/time balance. Deterministic tie‑breaking and small bounded local search keep results reproducible and runtime predictable.
experience: Favor small, Pareto-aware shortlists and CELF lazy-greedy with cached singleton HVs. Use vectorized box/scalar proxies to rank candidates, selectively cache pairwise HVs only when list ≤ ~40, enforce an HV-call budget, and prune using cheap upper bounds. Preallocate buffers and minimize vstack/copy inside hot loops; prefer deterministic seeds and tie-breakers. These choices give best hypervolume per HV call.
current_reflection: Favor small, Pareto-aware shortlists and CELF lazy-greedy with cached singleton HVs. Use vectorized box/scalar proxies to rank candidates, selectively cache pairwise HVs only when list ≤ ~40, enforce an HV-call budget, and prune using cheap upper bounds. Preallocate buffers and minimize vstack/copy inside hot loops; prefer deterministic seeds and tie-breakers. These choices give best hypervolume per HV call.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.

Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.
bad_reflections: Cache singleton HVs, precompute diffs/box volumes, and use sets/preallocated arrays to avoid Python-level copying and membership costs. Use CELF lazy updates with tight upper bounds (box or scalarized) and an explicit HV-call budget. Prefer a small, well-chosen shortlist (O(k)) with moderate weight vectors; do selective pairwise caching only when shortlist ≤ ~40. Tune swap and shortlist sizes as the primary knobs for quality vs cost.

Always prefilter to nondominated points, vectorize box/weight scores, and build an O(k) shortlist. Cache singleton HVs (and pairwise only when shortlist≤~40). Use CELF lazy greedy with timestamps and box‑based upper bounds, limit total HV calls, maintain selected points in a preallocated array to avoid vstack churn, and use sets/masks for membership. Finish with a tiny bounded deterministic swap pass. Consider lightweight scalar HVC approximations if HV calls must be avoided.

Favor deterministic shortlists, cheap vectorized proxies, cached singleton (and tiny pairwise) HVs, and CELF lazy greedy with tight upper‑bound pruning and an explicit HV‑call budget. Add a small, cached incremental swap pass. Prioritize data reuse (precompute diffs/scores), avoid Python-level remove/linear searches in hot loops, and keep shortlist sizes O(k) to maximize HV value per expensive call.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: small O(k) shortlist; CELF lazy-greedy; cached singleton HVs; vectorized box/scalar proxies; selective pairwise cache (≤40); HV-call budget; deterministic seeds; preallocated buffers.
- Advice: Build an O(k) shortlist via cheap vectorized proxies, always cache singleton HVCs, run CELF with timestamps and box upper bounds, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, enforce an HV-call budget and finish with a tiny deterministic bounded swap pass; preallocate arrays and avoid vstack/copies.
- Avoid: unbounded HV calls or pairwise caches, nested HV recomputations, Python-side per-candidate HVs in hot loops, nondeterministic tie-breaking, and frequent array copying.
- Explanation: These practices maximize hypervolume gain per expensive HV call, cut Python overhead, and yield fast, reproducible k‑subset selection.
